era_context: Classical Greece in the early 4th century BCE, in the immediate aftermath of the King's Peace (387 BCE). The Persian Empire is at the height of its influence over Greek affairs. Sparta, though weakened by the Corinthian War, remains a major power. In China, the Warring States period continues; in Italy, Rome is expanding its influence in Latium.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and early Silk Road networks

events.title: Agesilaus II of Sparta campaigns in Asia Minor
events.description: Agesilaus II, king of Sparta, continues his military campaigns in Asia Minor against Persian forces and their Greek allies. Though the King's Peace of 387 BCE nominally ended Greek-Persian conflict, Agesilaus maintains pressure on Persian-controlled territories. His campaigns are part of a broader Spartan strategy to maintain influence in the Eastern Mediterranean, though they will ultimately prove unsustainable given Sparta's declining resources and the rise of rival Greek powers.
events.certainty_note: Agesilaus's campaigns in Asia Minor are well-attested, but the precise chronology of individual campaigns in the 390s–380s BCE is uncertain. 386 BCE is assigned within the broader period of his activity.
cross_references: Leads to -371-001 (Battle of Leuctra, which ends Spartan dominance)

events.title: Evagoras I of Cyprus maintains independence against Persian pressure
events.description: Evagoras I, king of Salamis in Cyprus, continues to maintain a precarious independence from Persian control. Though nominally a Persian vassal, Evagoras cultivates ties with Athens and other Greek powers, attempting to balance Persian pressure with Greek support. His reign represents the complex position of Cypriot rulers in the 4th century BCE, caught between Persian imperial authority and Greek cultural and political influence.
events.certainty_note: Evagoras's reign is attested in Isocrates's contemporary oration, but the specific events of 386 BCE are not sharply defined. The year is assigned within the broader period of his rule (c. 410–374 BCE).
cross_references: Leads to -374-001 (Evagoras's death and Persian reassertion)

events.title: Thebes begins to emerge as a major power in Greece
events.description: In the aftermath of the King's Peace, Thebes begins to consolidate power in Boeotia and assert itself as a major Greek power. The Theban oligarchy, supported by a growing military establishment, starts to challenge Spartan hegemony. This period marks the beginning of Thebes's rise to prominence, which will culminate in the Battle of Leuctra (371 BCE) and the brief Theban hegemony of the 360s BCE.
events.certainty_note: Thebes's emergence as a power is a gradual process; 386 BCE is assigned as a point within this broader trajectory. The specific events of 386 BCE are not sharply documented.
cross_references: Leads to -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

disconfirming_evidence: The specific events of 386 BCE are not well-documented in surviving sources. The year falls within broader periods of activity (Agesilaus's campaigns, Evagoras's reign, Thebes's emergence) but no contemporary source marks 386 BCE as a turning point or significant year. The assignment of events to this year is somewhat arbitrary, based on historiographic convention and the broader context of the period. No disconfirming evidence has been identified for the events listed, but their significance specifically to 386 BCE (rather than the broader 390s–380s BCE period) is not established.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 386 BCE is moderate but sparse in terms of specific dated events. Greek sources (Xenophon, Isocrates, later compilations) provide context for the period, but precise chronology is often uncertain. The year falls in a period of relative stability after the King's Peace, before the major upheavals of the 370s BCE. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military history in Greece and the Eastern Mediterranean; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Persian sources are sparse. Events in other regions (Italy, Egypt, the broader Mediterranean) are poorly documented.

graph_edges.note: Agesilaus's continued campaigns in Asia Minor and the Aegean contribute to Spartan overextension, which is exposed by Thebes's victory at Leuctra.
Evagoras's reign continues until his death in 374 BCE; 386 BCE is a point within this period.
Thebes's consolidation of power in the 380s BCE directly leads to its military victory at Leuctra.
